title: Very High-Risk ASCVD Dyslipidemia Control
subtitle: Aggressive Multi-Pathway LDL Lowering — AHA/ACC, ESC/EAS & LAI Targets, Advanced Lipoprotein Testing & Stepwise Pharmacotherapy
authors: User-provided clinical summary; refs: Grundy et al. 2019, Misra et al. 2022, Zeitouni et al. 2020, Pradhan et al. 2020, Schwartz et al. 2023, Sayah et al. 2024

relatedChapters.title: Pattern B LDL: Small, Dense LDL & Dietary Strategy
Statins & Fenofibrate in Diabetic Macular Edema
Primary-Prevention Lipids & Aspirin in an Indian Diabetic

items.title: Why do standard lipid panels underestimate atherogenic burden in very high-risk ASCVD?
items.content:
Achieving complete dyslipidemia control in established ASCVD with very high-risk profiles requires an aggressive, multi-pathway strategy — not a single LDL number on a fasting panel.

Standard lipid panels frequently underestimate true atherogenic burden, particularly when metabolic discordance is present. This discordance — LDL-C at apparent target while particle number or inflammatory risk remains elevated — is a common feature of the South Asian phenotype (high triglycerides, low HDL, small dense LDL).

Complete control therefore demands guideline-tier risk classification, dual numeric targets (LDL-C plus non-HDL-C and ApoB), sequential pharmacotherapy, and advanced lipoprotein testing when discordance is suspected.
keyPoints: Very high-risk ASCVD needs multi-pathway control, not LDL alone
Standard panels miss discordant residual particle risk
South Asian phenotype: high TG, low HDL, sdLDL — Friedewald LDL-C may mislead
items.reference: Overview: "Standard lipid panels frequently underestimate the true atherogenic burden, particularly when metabolic discordance is present—a common feature in the South Asian phenotype."

items.title: How does AHA/ACC define very high cardiovascular risk in secondary prevention?
items.content:
Before initiating the treatment algorithm, classify the patient's exact risk tier.

AHA/ACC very high risk = either:
• A history of multiple major ASCVD events (e.g., recent ACS, prior MI, ischemic stroke, symptomatic PAD), OR
• One major ASCVD event plus multiple high-risk conditions (e.g., age ≥65, heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, diabetes, hypertension, CKD).

This tier justifies maximal statin intensity and early non-statin add-on when targets are unmet — the clinical mistake is treating a recurrent-event patient as "already on a statin, so we're done."
keyPoints: Multiple major ASCVD events = very high risk
One event + multiple comorbid risk factors = very high risk
Risk tier drives intensity of LDL lowering and add-on therapy
items.reference: 1. Define and Stratify "Very High Risk": "A history of multiple major ASCVD events (e.g., recent ACS, history of MI, ischemic stroke, symptomatic PAD) OR one major ASCVD event and multiple high-risk conditions (e.g., age >= 65, heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, diabetes, hypertension, CKD) (Grundy et al., 2019)."

items.title: How do LAI Extreme Risk Categories A and B refine targets beyond generic very high risk?
items.content:
The Lipid Association of India (LAI) adds an "extreme risk" stratum within established ASCVD — particularly relevant in Indian practice.

Category A: ASCVD + diabetes, OR ASCVD + familial hypercholesterolemia, OR premature ASCVD.

Category B: Recurrent ACS within years despite being on statins, OR ASCVD + polyvascular disease.

Category A mandates LDL-C <50 mg/dL (with optional ≤30 mg/dL). Category B mandates LDL-C ≤30 mg/dL — the most aggressive LDL floor in mainstream global guidance for a defined subgroup. Misclassifying a Category B patient as "LDL 48 = success" leaves substantial residual event risk.
keyPoints: LAI Category A: ASCVD + DM, FH, or premature ASCVD
LAI Category B: recurrent ACS on statins or polyvascular ASCVD
Category B LDL goal ≤30 mg/dL — lower than ESC's default <55 mg/dL
items.reference: LAI Extreme Risk Categories: "Category A: ASCVD + Diabetes, or ASCVD + familial hypercholesterolemia, or premature ASCVD. Category B: Recurrent ACS within years despite being on statins, or ASCVD + polyvascular disease (Misra et al., 2022)."

items.title: Why must LDL-C targets be pushed well below traditional thresholds in very high-risk ASCVD?
items.content:
To halt plaque progression and induce regression, targets must sit well below legacy "LDL <100" or even "LDL <70" thresholds.

ESC/EAS (very high risk): ≥50% reduction from baseline AND absolute LDL-C <55 mg/dL.

LAI extreme risk: LDL-C <50 mg/dL (Category A) and ≤30 mg/dL (Category B).

Secondary target — non-HDL-C: typically <85 mg/dL (or <55 mg/dL in LAI Extreme Category B); captures VLDL and IDL remnants not reflected in LDL-C alone.

Tertiary target — ApoB: <65 mg/dL; superior to LDL-C for assessing total atherogenic particle number and resolving LDL-C/ApoB discordance.
keyPoints: ESC: ≥50% LDL reduction + LDL-C <55 mg/dL
LAI extreme A: <50 mg/dL; extreme B: ≤30 mg/dL
Non-HDL-C and ApoB are co-primary/secondary targets for complete control
items.reference: 2. Establish Aggressive Primary and Secondary Targets: "ESC Goal: >= 50% reduction from baseline AND an absolute LDL-C of < 55 mg/dL (Zeitouni et al., 2020). LAI Goal: < 50 mg/dL for Extreme Risk Category A, and < 30 mg/dL for Extreme Risk Category B (Misra et al., 2022)."

items.title: Why is ApoB superior to LDL-C when assessing discordant residual risk?
items.content:
ApoB measures the exact number of atherogenic lipoprotein particles — VLDL, IDL, LDL, and Lp(a) — each carrying one ApoB molecule.

In metabolic syndrome, high triglycerides, or South Asian dyslipidemia, calculated LDL-C (Friedewald) can underestimate true particle burden: LDL-C may appear at target while ApoB remains elevated.

Action rule: if LDL-C is at target but ApoB remains >65 mg/dL, therapy must be intensified. ApoB is the primary marker for resolving ASCVD risk discordance and should trigger PCSK9 inhibitor consideration even when the standard panel looks "controlled."
keyPoints: ApoB = particle count across all atherogenic classes
Discordance: LDL-C at goal, ApoB elevated — undertreated
ApoB >65 mg/dL despite LDL at target → intensify therapy
items.reference: Apolipoprotein B (ApoB): "Utility: Measures the exact number of atherogenic particles (VLDL, IDL, LDL, Lp(a)). Action: If LDL-C is at target but ApoB remains elevated (> 65 mg/dL), therapy must be intensified. This is the primary marker for resolving ASCVD risk discordance."

items.title: How should elevated lipoprotein(a) change management when specific Lp(a)-lowering drugs are unavailable?
items.content:
Lp(a) is genetically determined, highly atherogenic, and pro-thrombotic. Guidelines recommend measuring it at least once in every adult's lifetime.

Levels >50 mg/dL (or >125 nmol/L) act as a major risk enhancer. Dedicated Lp(a)-lowering therapies (e.g., pelacarsen) remain in trials.

Clinical action today: elevated Lp(a) justifies earlier and more aggressive use of PCSK9 inhibitors (which lower Lp(a) by ~20–30%) and meticulous control of all other modifiable risk factors. Do not dismiss an elevated Lp(a) as "untreatable" — it is a mandate to drive LDL-C, ApoB, BP, and inflammation lower than you otherwise would.
keyPoints: Measure Lp(a) once in adulthood; >50 mg/dL or >125 nmol/L = risk enhancer
No approved Lp(a)-specific drug yet — manage aggressively around it
PCSK9 inhibitors lower Lp(a) ~20–30% and should be considered earlier
items.reference: Lipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)] via Immunoassay: "Levels > 50 mg/dL (or > 125 nmol/L) act as a major risk enhancer. While specific Lp(a)-lowering therapies (like Pelacarsen) are still in trials, elevated Lp(a) justifies earlier and more aggressive use of PCSK9 inhibitors (which lower Lp(a) by ~20-30%) and meticulous control of all other risk factors."

items.title: How do NMR or ion mobility assays help when standard LDL-C calculations fail?
items.content:
NMR spectroscopy and ion mobility directly quantify LDL particle number (LDL-P) and categorize particle size (e.g., small dense LDL).

These assays confirm atherogenic burden in complex cardiometabolic patients where elevated triglycerides skew standard LDL-C calculations — the Friedewald equation systematically underestimates LDL-C when TG >150 mg/dL and can mask high particle number.

Use advanced particle testing when: TG elevated, LDL-C/ApoB discordant, South Asian phenotype with "normal" LDL but high event rate, or residual events despite apparent LDL at goal.
keyPoints: NMR/ion mobility measure LDL-P and particle size directly
Friedewald LDL-C fails when TG elevated — underestimates risk
Order when discordance, high TG, or events despite "at-goal" LDL-C
items.reference: NMR / Ion Mobility (Particle Quantification): "Utility: Directly measures Total LDL-P and categorizes particle size (e.g., small dense LDL). Action: Confirms atherogenic burden in complex cardio-metabolic patients where triglyceride levels skew standard LDL-C calculations (Friedewald equation failures)."

items.title: Step 1 — Maximally tolerated high-intensity statin: doses and reassessment interval
items.content:
The foundation of the stepwise algorithm is sequential lipid-lowering therapy to reach aggressive targets.

Step 1: Initiate maximally tolerated high-intensity statin — Rosuvastatin 20–40 mg or Atorvastatin 40–80 mg.

Assess lipid response at 4–12 weeks. The common pitfall is accepting submaximal statin dosing in a very high-risk patient because "they had a side effect once on simvastatin 10 mg" without retrying a different high-intensity agent or alternate-day strategy.
keyPoints: Rosuvastatin 20–40 mg or atorvastatin 40–80 mg first line
Reassess LDL-C at 4–12 weeks
Submaximal statin dosing is the most common reason targets are missed
items.reference: Step 1: Maximally Tolerated High-Intensity Statin: "Initiate Rosuvastatin (20–40 mg) or Atorvastatin (40–80 mg). Assess response in 4–12 weeks."

items.title: Why add ezetimibe when LDL-C remains at or above 55 mg/dL on maximal statin?
items.content:
If LDL-C remains above target (e.g., ≥55 mg/dL) despite maximal statin therapy, add ezetimibe 10 mg.

This combination yields an additional 15–20% LDL-C reduction beyond statin alone and improves major cardiovascular outcomes — the IMPROVE-IT trial established outcome benefit for statin + ezetimibe in post-ACS patients.

Do not jump to PCSK9 inhibitors while ezetimibe has not been tried; ezetimibe is inexpensive, well tolerated, and outcome-proven as the mandatory Step 2 in the algorithm.
keyPoints: Add ezetimibe 10 mg when LDL ≥55 mg/dL on max statin
Additional 15–20% LDL-C lowering
IMPROVE-IT: outcome benefit — Step 2 before PCSK9i
items.reference: Step 2: Cholesterol Absorption Inhibitor: "If LDL-C remains above target (e.g., >= 55 mg/dL) despite maximal statin therapy, add Ezetimibe (10 mg). This combination yields an additional 15–20% LDL-C reduction and improves major cardiovascular outcomes (Pradhan et al., 2020)."

items.title: Why escalate to PCSK9 inhibition when statin plus ezetimibe still miss extreme-risk targets?
items.content:
If goals remain unmet on statin + ezetimibe, initiate a PCSK9 inhibitor (evolocumab or alirocumab). Extremely low LDL-C targets achieved via PCSK9 inhibition deliver sustained cardiovascular risk reduction in outcome trials (FOURIER, ODYSSEY OUTCOMES).

Schwartz et al. (2023) showed that transiently achieved very low LDL-C levels by statin and alirocumab after ACS are associated with cardiovascular risk reduction in ODYSSEY OUTCOMES — supporting treat-to-extreme-target in the highest-risk cohort.

Alternatively, inclisiran (siRNA, twice-yearly dosing) suits patients with adherence barriers. Category B LAI patients (LDL goal ≤30 mg/dL) will almost always require this step.
keyPoints: PCSK9i after failure of statin + ezetimibe
ODYSSEY OUTCOMES: very low LDL-C → sustained CV risk reduction
Inclisiran option when adherence is the barrier
items.reference: Step 3: PCSK9 Directed Therapy: "If the goal is still unmet on Statin + Ezetimibe, initiate a PCSK9 inhibitor (Evolocumab or Alirocumab). Extremely low LDL-C targets achieved via PCSK9 inhibition have been firmly established to deliver sustained cardiovascular risk reduction (Schwartz et al., 2023)."

items.title: How does bempedoic acid fit the algorithm for statin-intolerant very high-risk patients?
items.content:
Bempedoic acid is an ATP citrate lyase inhibitor useful as an adjunct — particularly in statin-intolerant patients who cannot reach Step 1 intensity.

It provides incremental LDL-C lowering (~15–25%) without the myopathic pathway of statins (prodrug activated only in liver, not muscle).

In statin-intolerant very high-risk ASCVD, combine bempedoic acid + ezetimibe as the floor, then add PCSK9 inhibitor if targets remain unmet. Do not leave statin-intolerant patients on ezetimibe monotherapy alone.
keyPoints: Bempedoic acid: ACL inhibitor, liver-selective prodrug
Key role in statin intolerance — adjunct to ezetimibe
Still escalate to PCSK9i if combined oral therapy misses target
items.reference: Step 4: Alternate / Emerging Pathways: "Bempedoic Acid: An ATP citrate lyase inhibitor useful as an adjunct, particularly in statin-intolerant patients."

items.title: When should icosapent ethyl be added beyond maximal statin therapy?
items.content:
Complete dyslipidemia control extends beyond the LDL axis.

If triglycerides remain elevated (135–499 mg/dL) despite maximally tolerated statins, add highly purified EPA — icosapent ethyl 2 g twice daily. REDUCE-IT demonstrated significant cardiovascular event reduction in this TG range, especially in post-ACS patients.

Pitfall: do not substitute mixed omega-3 supplements or dietary fish oil — outcome benefit is specific to prescription icosapent ethyl at 4 g/day total. Check for atrial fibrillation risk and bleeding context when prescribing.
keyPoints: TG 135–499 mg/dL on max statin → icosapent ethyl 2 g BID
REDUCE-IT: CV event reduction in statin-treated patients
Prescription EPA only — not OTC mixed omega-3
items.reference: Icosapent Ethyl: "If triglycerides remain elevated (135–499 mg/dL) despite maximally tolerated statins, the addition of highly purified EPA (Icosapent ethyl 2g twice daily) significantly reduces cardiovascular events, especially in post-ACS patients (Sayah et al., 2024)."

items.title: Why consider low-dose colchicine for residual inflammatory risk in established ASCVD?
items.content:
Even with LDL-C at aggressive targets, residual inflammatory risk (hs-CRP >2.0 mg/L) drives recurrent events — the CANTOS trial established that inflammation is an independent therapeutic target.

Consider low-dose colchicine 0.5 mg daily in established ASCVD with elevated hs-CRP. LoDoCo2 and COLCOT demonstrated benefit in chronic coronary disease and post-MI settings respectively.

Colchicine is not a substitute for LDL lowering — it addresses the inflammatory residual risk layer after lipids are maximally treated.
keyPoints: hs-CRP >2.0 mg/L = residual inflammatory risk despite lipid control
Colchicine 0.5 mg daily in established ASCVD
Anti-inflammatory add-on — not a replacement for LDL-lowering
items.reference: Colchicine: "Consider low-dose Colchicine (0.5 mg daily) to target residual inflammatory risk (hs-CRP > 2.0 mg/L) in established ASCVD."

items.title: Integrated stepwise approach — from risk tier to complete dyslipidemia control
items.content:
1. Stratify: AHA/ACC very high risk → LAI extreme A or B (sets LDL floor: <55, <50, or ≤30 mg/dL).
2. Set dual targets: LDL-C (primary) + non-HDL-C and ApoB (secondary/tertiary).
3. Stepwise pharmacotherapy: max statin → ezetimibe → PCSK9i/inclisiran → bempedoic acid if statin-intolerant.
4. Advanced testing: ApoB, Lp(a) once, NMR/ion mobility if discordant or high TG.
5. Beyond LDL: icosapent ethyl if TG 135–499 mg/dL; colchicine if hs-CRP elevated.

The South Asian phenotype demands ApoB and particle testing — treating LDL-C alone leaves discordant residual risk unaddressed.
keyPoints: Risk tier → target → sequential drugs → advanced testing → residual risk
Never stop at statin monotherapy in extreme-risk ASCVD
ApoB discordance resolution is the marker of complete control
items.reference: Overview: "Achieving complete control of dyslipidemia in patients with established Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease (ASCVD) and very high-risk profiles requires an aggressive, multi-pathway strategy."

items.question: A 58-year-old man with prior MI, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension is on atorvastatin 40 mg. LDL-C is 62 mg/dL. Per ESC/EAS very high-risk targets, what is the next appropriate lipid goal?
options: LDL-C <100 mg/dL — he is already at goal
LDL-C <70 mg/dL only — no percent reduction required
≥50% LDL reduction from baseline AND LDL-C <55 mg/dL
items.explanation: ESC/EAS very high-risk secondary prevention requires BOTH ≥50% reduction from baseline AND absolute LDL-C <55 mg/dL. LDL 62 mg/dL may still fail one or both criteria depending on baseline.
items.reference: 2. Establish Aggressive Primary and Secondary Targets: "ESC Goal: >= 50% reduction from baseline AND an absolute LDL-C of < 55 mg/dL (Zeitouni et al., 2020)."

items.question: A 52-year-old with CAD, diabetes, and polyvascular disease (CAD + symptomatic PAD) is classified under LAI extreme risk. What LDL-C target applies?
items.explanation: ASCVD + polyvascular disease = LAI Extreme Risk Category B, with LDL-C goal ≤30 mg/dL and non-HDL-C <55 mg/dL.
items.reference: LAI Extreme Risk Categories: "Category B: Recurrent ACS within years despite being on statins, or ASCVD + polyvascular disease (Misra et al., 2022)."

items.question: A post-ACS patient on rosuvastatin 40 mg has LDL-C 78 mg/dL at 8 weeks. Ezetimibe has not been started. What is the guideline-directed next step?
options: Switch to low-intensity statin for tolerability
Add ezetimibe 10 mg and recheck in 4–12 weeks
Start PCSK9 inhibitor immediately without ezetimibe
items.explanation: Step 2 in the algorithm: add ezetimibe when LDL remains above target on maximal statin. PCSK9 inhibitors are Step 3, after statin + ezetimibe failure.
items.reference: Step 2: Cholesterol Absorption Inhibitor: "If LDL-C remains above target (e.g., >= 55 mg/dL) despite maximal statin therapy, add Ezetimibe (10 mg)."

items.question: A South Asian patient with metabolic syndrome has LDL-C 52 mg/dL (apparently at ESC goal) but ApoB 92 mg/dL. What is the correct management response?
options: No change — LDL-C is at target
Intensify lipid-lowering therapy to lower ApoB below 65 mg/dL
Repeat fasting lipid panel in 12 months only
items.explanation: LDL-C/ApoB discordance means undertreatment. ApoB >65 mg/dL despite LDL at target mandates therapy intensification — typically adding ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitor.
items.reference: Apolipoprotein B (ApoB): "If LDL-C is at target but ApoB remains elevated (> 65 mg/dL), therapy must be intensified. This is the primary marker for resolving ASCVD risk discordance."

items.question: A 45-year-old with premature MI has Lp(a) 180 nmol/L on one-time screening. No Lp(a)-lowering drug is available. Best evidence-based approach?
options: Aggressive LDL-C/ApoB lowering including earlier PCSK9 inhibitor consideration
Start statin solely to lower Lp(a)
Repeat Lp(a) monthly to track response
items.explanation: Elevated Lp(a) is a risk enhancer justifying more aggressive LDL lowering and earlier PCSK9i (which lowers Lp(a) ~20–30%). Lp(a) is genetically stable — no need for serial monitoring.
items.reference: Lipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)] via Immunoassay: "elevated Lp(a) justifies earlier and more aggressive use of PCSK9 inhibitors (which lower Lp(a) by ~20-30%) and meticulous control of all other risk factors."

items.question: A patient 6 months post-ACS is on high-intensity statin with LDL-C 48 mg/dL but triglycerides 220 mg/dL. Most appropriate add-on per REDUCE-IT criteria?
items.explanation: TG 135–499 mg/dL on max statin in a post-ACS patient: icosapent ethyl 2 g BID (REDUCE-IT). OTC fish oil and fibrate outcomes differ from prescription EPA.
items.reference: Icosapent Ethyl: "If triglycerides remain elevated (135–499 mg/dL) despite maximally tolerated statins, the addition of highly purified EPA (Icosapent ethyl 2g twice daily) significantly reduces cardiovascular events, especially in post-ACS patients (Sayah et al., 2024)."

items.question: A statin-intolerant patient with established ASCVD cannot tolerate any statin dose. LDL-C remains 110 mg/dL on ezetimibe 10 mg alone. Next best step?
options: Accept LDL 110 — ezetimibe is maximum therapy
Add bempedoic acid and reassess; escalate to PCSK9 inhibitor if still above target
Switch ezetimibe to bile acid sequestrant only
items.explanation: Bempedoic acid is the Step 4 adjunct for statin-intolerant patients. If combined oral therapy fails, PCSK9 inhibitor is still indicated in very high-risk ASCVD.
items.reference: Step 4: Alternate / Emerging Pathways: "Bempedoic Acid: An ATP citrate lyase inhibitor useful as an adjunct, particularly in statin-intolerant patients."

items.question: For LAI Extreme Risk Category B, which non-HDL-C target applies alongside LDL-C ≤30 mg/dL?
items.explanation: LAI Extreme Category B pairs LDL-C ≤30 mg/dL with non-HDL-C <55 mg/dL — capturing remnant particles when LDL is driven to ultralow levels.
items.reference: 2. Establish Aggressive Primary and Secondary Targets: "Secondary Target (Non-HDL-C): Goal is typically < 85 mg/dL (or < 55 mg/dL in LAI Extreme Category B)."

items.question: Apolipoprotein B measures the total number of atherogenic lipoprotein particles, making it superior to LDL-C for detecting discordant residual risk.
items.explanation: Each VLDL, IDL, LDL, and Lp(a) particle carries one ApoB molecule. Particle count can remain elevated when calculated LDL-C appears at goal.
items.reference: Apolipoprotein B (ApoB): "Utility: Measures the exact number of atherogenic particles (VLDL, IDL, LDL, Lp(a))."

items.question: Adding ezetimibe 10 mg to maximal statin therapy typically lowers LDL-C by an additional 15–20% and improves cardiovascular outcomes.
items.explanation: Ezetimibe is the mandatory Step 2 before PCSK9 inhibitors; IMPROVE-IT demonstrated outcome benefit in post-ACS patients.
items.reference: Step 2: Cholesterol Absorption Inhibitor: "This combination yields an additional 15–20% LDL-C reduction and improves major cardiovascular outcomes (Pradhan et al., 2020)."

items.question: Low-dose colchicine (0.5 mg daily) should replace statin therapy when hs-CRP is elevated in established ASCVD.
items.explanation: Colchicine targets residual inflammatory risk as an add-on when hs-CRP >2.0 mg/L — it does not replace LDL-lowering therapy.
items.reference: Colchicine: "Consider low-dose Colchicine (0.5 mg daily) to target residual inflammatory risk (hs-CRP > 2.0 mg/L) in established ASCVD."

items.question: Lp(a) should be measured at least once in every adult's lifetime because it is genetically determined and acts as a major risk enhancer when elevated.
items.explanation: Lp(a) is ~90% genetically determined, stable over life, and levels >50 mg/dL or >125 nmol/L enhance ASCVD risk.
items.reference: Lipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)] via Immunoassay: "Guidelines recommend checking at least once in every adult's lifetime."

items.assertion: Therapy must be intensified when LDL-C is at target but ApoB remains above 65 mg/dL in a very high-risk ASCVD patient.
items.reason: ApoB measures the total number of atherogenic particles and is the primary marker for resolving LDL-C/ApoB discordance.
items.explanation: Both true and causally linked — discordant elevation of ApoB despite LDL at goal indicates undertreatment by particle number.
items.reference: Apolipoprotein B (ApoB): "If LDL-C is at target but ApoB remains elevated (> 65 mg/dL), therapy must be intensified. This is the primary marker for resolving ASCVD risk discordance."

items.assertion: PCSK9 inhibitors should be initiated before ezetimibe in a post-ACS patient whose LDL-C remains ≥55 mg/dL on maximal statin.
items.reason: Ezetimibe adds only 15–20% LDL reduction and has no outcome data.
items.explanation: Assertion is false — ezetimibe is Step 2 before PCSK9i. Reason is also false — ezetimibe has outcome benefit (IMPROVE-IT).
items.reference: Step 2: Cholesterol Absorption Inhibitor: "If LDL-C remains above target (e.g., >= 55 mg/dL) despite maximal statin therapy, add Ezetimibe (10 mg)."

items.assertion: Icosapent ethyl should be considered when triglycerides are 135–499 mg/dL despite maximally tolerated statin in established ASCVD.
items.reason: Highly purified EPA significantly reduces cardiovascular events in this triglyceride range, especially post-ACS (REDUCE-IT).
items.explanation: Both true and causally linked — REDUCE-IT established outcome benefit for icosapent ethyl in statin-treated patients with TG 135–499 mg/dL.
items.reference: Icosapent Ethyl: "If triglycerides remain elevated (135–499 mg/dL) despite maximally tolerated statins, the addition of highly purified EPA (Icosapent ethyl 2g twice daily) significantly reduces cardiovascular events, especially in post-ACS patients (Sayah et al., 2024)."
